title: Sepsis — ICU Management
oneLiner: Life-threatening organ dysfunction due to dysregulated host response to infection; ICU admission required for organ support and source control.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Refractory hypotension (distributive shock):** MAP persistently <65 despite adequate fluid resuscitation → needs vasopressors immediately, not more fluid. **Single finding:** vasopressor requirement escalating >0.1 mcg/kg/min noradrenaline + still MAP <65.
- **Severe hypoxaemia (ARDS):** PaO2/FiO2 <150 on ≥40% O2 → consider early intubation, prone positioning, neuromuscular blockade. **Single finding:** O2 sat <90% on a non-rebreather mask despite high FiO2.
- **Hyperlactataemia >4 mmol/L:** Indicates tissue hypoperfusion or mitochondrial dysfunction; every hour of unresolved hyperlactataemia increases mortality. **Single finding:** lactate rising on serial ABGs despite initiation of antibiotics + fluids.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Fever or hypothermia (<36°C)
  - Tachypnoea, tachycardia, hypotension
  - Altered mental state (confusion, drowsiness)
  - Mottled skin, prolonged capillary refill >3s
  - Elevated lactate on arterial blood gas
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Source: lung (crepitations, purulent sputum), abdomen (tenderness, distension), urine (suprapubic pain, flank tenderness), skin (cellulitis, wound infection), line (tenderness, discharge)
  - Signs of poor perfusion: cool peripheries, reduced urine output <0.5 mL/kg/h
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Septic shock (most likely)** → vasopressor requirement + lactate >2
- **Cardiogenic shock** → high CVP, clear lungs, low cardiac output; look for acute coronary syndrome or arrhythmia
- **Hypovolaemic shock** → low CVP, flat neck veins, history of bleeding or diarrhoea
- **Anaphylaxis** → urticaria, wheeze, rapid onset after drug/food
- **Adrenal crisis** → hyperpigmentation, hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia
- **Splitter:** Lactate >4 + vasopressor requirement + distributive pattern (warm peripheries) → sepsis/pancreatitis vs cold peripheries + raised JVP → cardiogenic
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Arterial blood gas (lactate, pH, PaO2, PaCO2, HCO3)
  - Blood cultures (two sets, peripheral + line if present)
  - Urine output monitoring (catheter)
  - ECG (rule out ischaemia)
  - Point-of-care glucose, lactate
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, creatinine, LFTs, coagulation screen
  - CRP, procalcitonin (if available and local protocol)
  - Blood cultures ×2
  - Other cultures: sputum, urine, swabs, CSF as indicated
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: Chest X-ray (pneumonia, effusion)
  - Second-line: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis if source unknown (especially post-surgical, immunocompromised)
  - Ultrasound (echocardiography, abdominal US for biliary/cholecystitis, RUQ)
- **Special tests:**
  - Procalcitonin (not for diagnosis, but to guide de-escalation)
  - Biomarkers: endotoxin activity assay (if suspected gram-negative)
  - Source-specific: bronchoalveolar lavage, lumbar puncture, joint aspiration
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **qSOFA:** ≥2 of: RR≥22, SBP≤100, altered mental state – screen for sepsis
  - **SOFA score:** quantifies organ dysfunction (respiratory, cardiovascular, hepatic, coagulation, renal, neurological); ΔSOFA≥2 defines sepsis
  - **Surviving Sepsis Campaign (SSC) 1-hour bundle:** measure lactate, blood cultures, broad-spectrum antibiotics, start crystalloid at 30 mL/kg if hypotensive or lactate≥4, start vasopressors if MAP<65 despite fluids
- **Never-miss test:** **Blood cultures ×2 before antibiotics** – omission means you lose the chance to identify the causative organism and tailor therapy
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED/ICU admission):**
  - **1-hour bundle:** lactate, cultures, broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (within 1 hour), crystalloid 30 mL/kg if hypotensive/lactate≥4, vasopressors if MAP<65 despite fluids.
  - **Source control:** remove infected lines, drain abscess, surgical debridement – within 12 hours.
  - **Central venous access** for vasopressors and central venous pressure monitoring.
  - **Arterial line** for continuous blood pressure monitoring.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - **Fluid balance:** reassess after initial bolus; dynamic assessment (passive leg raise, pulse pressure variation). Avoid excessive fluids if no response.
  - **Vasopressors:** noradrenaline first-line (titrate to MAP≥65). Add vasopressin (0.03 U/min) or adrenaline if noradrenaline >0.5 mcg/kg/min.
  - **Inotropes:** dobutamine added if signs of myocardial dysfunction (low cardiac output, raised CVP, echocardiographic evidence).
  - **Steroids:** hydrocortisone 50 mg IV q6h + fludrocortisone if refractory shock despite adequate vasopressors (check local protocol).
  - **Antibiotic escalation/de-escalation:** daily review; stop empiric agents if culture negative and clinical improvement; narrow spectrum if organism identified.
  - **Organ support:** mechanical ventilation (lung protective: TV 6 mL/kg ideal body weight, plateau pressure ≤30), renal replacement therapy if AKI stage 2-3, glucose control (target 6-10 mmol/L).
  - **Nutrition:** start enteral nutrition within 48 hours if haemodynamically stable; early parenteral if contraindicated.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Post-ICU rehabilitation (physical, cognitive, psychological).
  - Follow-up for any residual organ dysfunction (e.g. renal, respiratory).
  - Review antibiotic duration: typically 7-10 days; longer for deep-seated infection.
- **Default Orders (if admitted to ICU):**
  - **Fluids:** IV crystalloid (Plasmalyte or Hartmann’s) – maintenance + boluses as needed (do not rely on total volume; reassess dynamically).
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (or adjusted for weight/renal function) if no contraindication.
  - **Diet/NBM status:** NBM until source control and airway secured, then start enteral feeds via NG/NJ as per critical care dietitian.
  - **Observation frequency:** continuous monitoring (ECG, SpO2, invasive BP, CVP) at least hourly, plus urine output hourly.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Empiric antibiotics (community-acquired sepsis):** piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g IV q6h *or* ceftriaxone 2 g IV daily + metronidazole 500 mg IV q8h (check local guidelines).
  - **Empiric antibiotics (hospital-acquired/ICU-acquired):** piperacillin-tazobactam or meropenem 1 g IV q8h (adjust for renal function). Add vancomycin (loading 25–30 mg/kg, then 15–20 mg/kg q12h) if MRSA risk.
  - **Noradrenaline:** start 0.05–0.1 mcg/kg/min IV infusion, titrate to MAP≥65 (max [check local policy]).
  - **Dobutamine:** 2.5–20 mcg/kg/min IV infusion (inodilator).
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Ceftazidime + gentamicin (if pseudomonas suspected).
  - Teicoplanin 400 mg IV q12h for 3 doses then daily (if renal impairment; MRSA/MSSA).
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Piperacillin-tazobactam: reduced clearance in renal failure; reduce dose if eGFR <20.
  - Vancomycin: monitor trough levels (target 15-20 mg/L); risk of nephrotoxicity if combined with aminoglycosides.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - All renally cleared antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam, meropenem, gentamicin, vancomycin) need dose adjustment per local renal protocol.
  - Noradrenaline – no renal adjustment; dose clinically.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Noradrenaline extravasation** – give via central line only; if peripheral in emergency, monitor site hourly.
  - **Gentamicin – missed timing of levels** – always check trough before second dose; nephrotoxic if accumulated.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Refractory shock (needs escalating vasopressors, consider stress-dose steroids)
  - ARDS (PaO2/FiO2 <200, need lung-protective ventilation)
  - Acute kidney injury (stage 2-3, need renal replacement therapy)
  - Disseminated intravascular coagulation (elevated D-dimer, low platelets, prolonged PT/APTT)
  - Stress ulceration (GI bleeding)
  - Hyperglycaemia / hypoglycaemia
  - Ventilator-associated pneumonia
  - Line-related bloodstream infection
- **Chronic:**
  - Post-ICU syndrome (critical illness polyneuropathy, cognitive decline, PTSD)
  - Chronic critical illness (prolonged ventilation, dependency)
  - Reduced quality of life (physical, psychological sequelae)
sections.Prognosis:
- ICU mortality for septic shock ~30-50% depending on comorbidities and source.
- Early recognition, timely antibiotics, and source control improve survival.
- Long-term: up to 50% of survivors have persistent organ dysfunction or cognitive impairment.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always perform a quick-look echocardiogram** after initial resuscitation to differentiate shock types and guide vasopressor/inotrope choice.
- **Use dynamic indices** (passive leg raise, pulse pressure variation) to guide fluid responsiveness – avoid blind fluid challenges.
- **Consider early advanced haemodynamic monitoring** (PiCCO, echocardiography) if shock persists – it changes management in ~50% of cases.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not delay antibiotics** for cultures if the patient is unstable – take cultures then immediately start antibiotics within 1 hour.
- **Do not give excessive fluids** (e.g., >2–3 L) without reassessing – risk of pulmonary oedema, abdominal compartment syndrome, and worsening oxygenation.
- **Do not stop vasopressors prematurely** – wean slowly as BP stabilises; abrupt cessation can cause rebound hypotension.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** fever, altered mental state, difficulty breathing, new confusion, low urine output (<500 mL/day), rising lactate on follow-up bloods.
- **Follow-up:** ICU step-down clinic at 2-4 weeks; primary care review at 1 week; ongoing management of any organ dysfunction.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **When to call senior / ICU / crash team:**
  - NEWS2 ≥7 or any red parameter (respiratory rate >30, SpO2 <90% on O2, systolic BP <90, lactate >4, GCS <9)
  - PaO2/FiO2 <150 despite 40% O2
  - Requirement for escalating noradrenaline >0.3 mcg/kg/min
  - Lactate rising despite initial resuscitation
  - Anuria or oliguria <0.3 mL/kg/h for >2 hours
  - Any new organ failure (e.g., AKI stage 2, new arrhythmia)

rawMarkdown:
## Sepsis — ICU Management
**One-liner:** Life-threatening organ dysfunction due to dysregulated host response to infection; ICU admission required for organ support and source control.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Refractory hypotension (distributive shock):** MAP persistently <65 despite adequate fluid resuscitation → needs vasopressors immediately, not more fluid. **Single finding:** vasopressor requirement escalating >0.1 mcg/kg/min noradrenaline + still MAP <65.
- **Severe hypoxaemia (ARDS):** PaO2/FiO2 <150 on ≥40% O2 → consider early intubation, prone positioning, neuromuscular blockade. **Single finding:** O2 sat <90% on a non-rebreather mask despite high FiO2.
- **Hyperlactataemia >4 mmol/L:** Indicates tissue hypoperfusion or mitochondrial dysfunction; every hour of unresolved hyperlactataemia increases mortality. **Single finding:** lactate rising on serial ABGs despite initiation of antibiotics + fluids.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Fever or hypothermia (<36°C)
  - Tachypnoea, tachycardia, hypotension
  - Altered mental state (confusion, drowsiness)
  - Mottled skin, prolonged capillary refill >3s
  - Elevated lactate on arterial blood gas
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Source: lung (crepitations, purulent sputum), abdomen (tenderness, distension), urine (suprapubic pain, flank tenderness), skin (cellulitis, wound infection), line (tenderness, discharge)
  - Signs of poor perfusion: cool peripheries, reduced urine output <0.5 mL/kg/h

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Septic shock (most likely)** → vasopressor requirement + lactate >2
- **Cardiogenic shock** → high CVP, clear lungs, low cardiac output; look for acute coronary syndrome or arrhythmia
- **Hypovolaemic shock** → low CVP, flat neck veins, history of bleeding or diarrhoea
- **Anaphylaxis** → urticaria, wheeze, rapid onset after drug/food
- **Adrenal crisis** → hyperpigmentation, hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia
- **Splitter:** Lactate >4 + vasopressor requirement + distributive pattern (warm peripheries) → sepsis/pancreatitis vs cold peripheries + raised JVP → cardiogenic

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Arterial blood gas (lactate, pH, PaO2, PaCO2, HCO3)
  - Blood cultures (two sets, peripheral + line if present)
  - Urine output monitoring (catheter)
  - ECG (rule out ischaemia)
  - Point-of-care glucose, lactate
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, creatinine, LFTs, coagulation screen
  - CRP, procalcitonin (if available and local protocol)
  - Blood cultures ×2
  - Other cultures: sputum, urine, swabs, CSF as indicated
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: Chest X-ray (pneumonia, effusion)
  - Second-line: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis if source unknown (especially post-surgical, immunocompromised)
  - Ultrasound (echocardiography, abdominal US for biliary/cholecystitis, RUQ)
- **Special tests:**
  - Procalcitonin (not for diagnosis, but to guide de-escalation)
  - Biomarkers: endotoxin activity assay (if suspected gram-negative)
  - Source-specific: bronchoalveolar lavage, lumbar puncture, joint aspiration
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **qSOFA:** ≥2 of: RR≥22, SBP≤100, altered mental state – screen for sepsis
  - **SOFA score:** quantifies organ dysfunction (respiratory, cardiovascular, hepatic, coagulation, renal, neurological); ΔSOFA≥2 defines sepsis
  - **Surviving Sepsis Campaign (SSC) 1-hour bundle:** measure lactate, blood cultures, broad-spectrum antibiotics, start crystalloid at 30 mL/kg if hypotensive or lactate≥4, start vasopressors if MAP<65 despite fluids
- **Never-miss test:** **Blood cultures ×2 before antibiotics** – omission means you lose the chance to identify the causative organism and tailor therapy

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED/ICU admission):**
  - **1-hour bundle:** lactate, cultures, broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (within 1 hour), crystalloid 30 mL/kg if hypotensive/lactate≥4, vasopressors if MAP<65 despite fluids.
  - **Source control:** remove infected lines, drain abscess, surgical debridement – within 12 hours.
  - **Central venous access** for vasopressors and central venous pressure monitoring.
  - **Arterial line** for continuous blood pressure monitoring.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - **Fluid balance:** reassess after initial bolus; dynamic assessment (passive leg raise, pulse pressure variation). Avoid excessive fluids if no response.
  - **Vasopressors:** noradrenaline first-line (titrate to MAP≥65). Add vasopressin (0.03 U/min) or adrenaline if noradrenaline >0.5 mcg/kg/min.
  - **Inotropes:** dobutamine added if signs of myocardial dysfunction (low cardiac output, raised CVP, echocardiographic evidence).
  - **Steroids:** hydrocortisone 50 mg IV q6h + fludrocortisone if refractory shock despite adequate vasopressors (check local protocol).
  - **Antibiotic escalation/de-escalation:** daily review; stop empiric agents if culture negative and clinical improvement; narrow spectrum if organism identified.
  - **Organ support:** mechanical ventilation (lung protective: TV 6 mL/kg ideal body weight, plateau pressure ≤30), renal replacement therapy if AKI stage 2-3, glucose control (target 6-10 mmol/L).
  - **Nutrition:** start enteral nutrition within 48 hours if haemodynamically stable; early parenteral if contraindicated.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Post-ICU rehabilitation (physical, cognitive, psychological).
  - Follow-up for any residual organ dysfunction (e.g. renal, respiratory).
  - Review antibiotic duration: typically 7-10 days; longer for deep-seated infection.
- **Default Orders (if admitted to ICU):**
  - **Fluids:** IV crystalloid (Plasmalyte or Hartmann’s) – maintenance + boluses as needed (do not rely on total volume; reassess dynamically).
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (or adjusted for weight/renal function) if no contraindication.
  - **Diet/NBM status:** NBM until source control and airway secured, then start enteral feeds via NG/NJ as per critical care dietitian.
  - **Observation frequency:** continuous monitoring (ECG, SpO2, invasive BP, CVP) at least hourly, plus urine output hourly.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Empiric antibiotics (community-acquired sepsis):** piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g IV q6h *or* ceftriaxone 2 g IV daily + metronidazole 500 mg IV q8h (check local guidelines).
  - **Empiric antibiotics (hospital-acquired/ICU-acquired):** piperacillin-tazobactam or meropenem 1 g IV q8h (adjust for renal function). Add vancomycin (loading 25–30 mg/kg, then 15–20 mg/kg q12h) if MRSA risk.
  - **Noradrenaline:** start 0.05–0.1 mcg/kg/min IV infusion, titrate to MAP≥65 (max [check local policy]).
  - **Dobutamine:** 2.5–20 mcg/kg/min IV infusion (inodilator).
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Ceftazidime + gentamicin (if pseudomonas suspected).
  - Teicoplanin 400 mg IV q12h for 3 doses then daily (if renal impairment; MRSA/MSSA).
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Piperacillin-tazobactam: reduced clearance in renal failure; reduce dose if eGFR <20.
  - Vancomycin: monitor trough levels (target 15-20 mg/L); risk of nephrotoxicity if combined with aminoglycosides.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - All renally cleared antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam, meropenem, gentamicin, vancomycin) need dose adjustment per local renal protocol.
  - Noradrenaline – no renal adjustment; dose clinically.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Noradrenaline extravasation** – give via central line only; if peripheral in emergency, monitor site hourly.
  - **Gentamicin – missed timing of levels** – always check trough before second dose; nephrotoxic if accumulated.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Refractory shock (needs escalating vasopressors, consider stress-dose steroids)
  - ARDS (PaO2/FiO2 <200, need lung-protective ventilation)
  - Acute kidney injury (stage 2-3, need renal replacement therapy)
  - Disseminated intravascular coagulation (elevated D-dimer, low platelets, prolonged PT/APTT)
  - Stress ulceration (GI bleeding)
  - Hyperglycaemia / hypoglycaemia
  - Ventilator-associated pneumonia
  - Line-related bloodstream infection
- **Chronic:**
  - Post-ICU syndrome (critical illness polyneuropathy, cognitive decline, PTSD)
  - Chronic critical illness (prolonged ventilation, dependency)
  - Reduced quality of life (physical, psychological sequelae)

### Prognosis
- ICU mortality for septic shock ~30-50% depending on comorbidities and source.
- Early recognition, timely antibiotics, and source control improve survival.
- Long-term: up to 50% of survivors have persistent organ dysfunction or cognitive impairment.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always perform a quick-look echocardiogram** after initial resuscitation to differentiate shock types and guide vasopressor/inotrope choice.
- **Use dynamic indices** (passive leg raise, pulse pressure variation) to guide fluid responsiveness – avoid blind fluid challenges.
- **Consider early advanced haemodynamic monitoring** (PiCCO, echocardiography) if shock persists – it changes management in ~50% of cases.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not delay antibiotics** for cultures if the patient is unstable – take cultures then immediately start antibiotics within 1 hour.
- **Do not give excessive fluids** (e.g., >2–3 L) without reassessing – risk of pulmonary oedema, abdominal compartment syndrome, and worsening oxygenation.
- **Do not stop vasopressors prematurely** – wean slowly as BP stabilises; abrupt cessation can cause rebound hypotension.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** fever, altered mental state, difficulty breathing, new confusion, low urine output (<500 mL/day), rising lactate on follow-up bloods.
- **Follow-up:** ICU step-down clinic at 2-4 weeks; primary care review at 1 week; ongoing management of any organ dysfunction.

### Escalation Criteria
- **When to call senior / ICU / crash team:**
  - NEWS2 ≥7 or any red parameter (respiratory rate >30, SpO2 <90% on O2, systolic BP <90, lactate >4, GCS <9)
  - PaO2/FiO2 <150 despite 40% O2
  - Requirement for escalating noradrenaline >0.3 mcg/kg/min
  - Lactate rising despite initial resuscitation
  - Anuria or oliguria <0.3 mL/kg/h for >2 hours
  - Any new organ failure (e.g., AKI stage 2, new arrhythmia)
